behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: Failure is at a module handoff: file discovery + filename-prefix parsing + execution sequencing. A pure Unit test of the sorter can miss bugs where discovered files are filtered/mapped incorrectly before execution. No behavior text requires real subprocess/OS timing/network, and no AI dependency. Rejected higher level (System): behavior only says order is determined by filename prefix, not real OS/subprocess behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Run the runner against a temp `migrations/` directory with out-of-order filenames (e.g., `002_b.sql`, `001_a.sql`, `010_c.sql`) and a local test PostgreSQL/schema; assert side effects show apply order `001,002,010` and `_migrations` records that same order.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: This is idempotency across runs via `_migrations` state handoff (runner write on run 1, runner read/skip on run 2). Mocking repo/storage can hide real schema/query mismatches, so Unit is too low. No required real subprocess/OS primitive beyond normal DB access; no AI output dependency. Rejected higher level (System): behavior text is about persisted migration state on subsequent run, not OS/subprocess/network fault behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Apply a migration once, then invoke runner again against same test DB; assert migration SQL side effect happens once and second run logs/records skip without re-execution.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: Behavior depends on transaction + runner control-flow interaction: DB rollback semantics and stop-on-failure orchestration. Unit tests with mocked transaction objects can miss real SQL/transaction boundary issues; integration with real Postgres semantics is needed. No quoted requirement for subprocess-level behavior beyond DB interaction, and no AI dependency. Rejected higher level (System): text requires rollback and halt in one run, not OS/subprocess behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Create migration N with multiple statements where one fails, followed by migration N+1. Run runner on test DB; assert statements from N are rolled back, N+1 is not attempted, and failed migration is not marked applied.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: Escalation phrase: "two instances of the runner start at the same time" and "across both instances". This requires real concurrent processes and race behavior at process/DB-lock boundary; in-process mocks often serialize and hide failures. No AI dependency, and not multi-agentic pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Launch two runner processes concurrently against the same Postgres DB and same migrations set; assert final schema/data corresponds to one execution per migration, `_migrations` has one row per migration, and no duplicate side effects.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the `_migrations` table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is initialization ordering between metadata bootstrap and migration executor modules on fresh DB state. Unit tests of table-creation helper alone miss ordering bugs in orchestrator. No explicit need for subprocess/OS behavior, no AI dependency. Rejected higher level (System): behavior concerns startup ordering in runner logic, not real OS/subprocess conditions.
behaviors.test_description: Run against an empty test DB with one migration file that depends on `_migrations` existing; assert `_migrations` is created first and migration then applies successfully.
